AQA
GCSE
ECONOMICS
8136/1
Paper 1 How Markets Work

Mark scheme

Predicted Paper · Hard
Version: 1.0 Final


Mark schemes are prepared by the Lead Assessment Writer and considered, together with the relevant
questions, by a panel of subject teachers.

This mark scheme is for a predicted paper in the style of AQA GCSE Economics 8136/1 (How Markets
Work). The format, command words, mark allocation and Assessment Objectives mirror the June 2024
paper.

It must be stressed that a mark scheme is a working document. Indicative content is provided as a guide for
examiners. It is not intended to be exhaustive and other valid points should be credited. Students do not
have to cover all of the points mentioned in the Indicative content to reach the highest level of the mark
scheme.

An answer which contains nothing of relevance to the question must be awarded no marks.
                                                    MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED

 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  11               State two possible differences between gross pay and net pay.                               2

                   AO1 = 2

                   1 mark for each correct difference (max 2).

                   Possible answers:
                    • gross pay is total earnings before deductions; net pay is the amount received after
                      deductions
                    • gross pay is shown as the headline salary/wage; net pay is the 'take-home pay'
                    • gross pay includes income tax and National Insurance; net pay has these (and other
                      deductions such as pension and student loan) removed
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     2
 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  12               Explain one impact of an increase in the number of firms in a market on the                 2
                   prices charged to consumers.

                   AO1 = 2

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      2         2      Possible impact on prices explained.

                      1         1      Possible impact on prices identified.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • greater competition forces firms to lower prices to attract customers
                    • individual firms have less market power, reducing their ability to charge above costs
                    • firms may need to be more efficient to survive, allowing lower prices
                    • in the short run, prices may fall but choice may also rise
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  13               Explain why a stock exchange is an example of a market.                                     2

                   AO1 = 2

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      2         2      Reason explained.

                      1         1      Reason identified.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • buyers (investors) and sellers (shareholders/firms) come into contact
                    • there is demand for shares (from investors) and supply of shares (from sellers/
                      issuers)
                    • prices are agreed between buyers and sellers, and change according to supply and
                      demand
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     3
                                                    MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  14      1        Calculate the total profit earned by the firm from selling 4 200 T-shirts.                  2

                   AO2 = 2
                    • Total revenue = £7.50 × 4 200 = £31 500
                    • Total variable costs = £3.20 × 4 200 = £13 440
                    • Total costs = £5 460 + £13 440 = £18 900
                    • Total profit = £31 500 – £18 900 = £12 600

                   1 mark for correctly calculating total costs or total revenue if final answer incorrect.

                   1 mark for correct method with correct figures but incorrect final answer.

                   2 marks for a correct answer with no working shown.


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  14      2        Explain one way the clothing firm could try to increase its profit without                  2
                   increasing the selling price.

                   AO1 = 2

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      2         2      Possible way explained.

                      1         1      Possible way identified.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • reduce variable costs (e.g. cheaper fabrics, bulk purchasing)
                    • reduce fixed costs (e.g. move to a cheaper factory / renegotiate rent)
                    • invest in more productive machinery to lower unit costs
                    • increase sales volume through advertising / new markets → spread fixed costs
                    • reduce waste / improve efficiency through training
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     4
 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  15               Analyse the possible impact of the fall in global oil prices shown in Figure 1              6
                   upon other markets such as airlines and road haulage.

                   AO2 = 2, AO3 = 4

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      3        5–6     A full analysis of the economic evidence, a well-developed and logical
                                       chain of reasoning.

                      2        3–4     An incomplete analysis of the economic evidence, starts to develop a
                                       chain of reasoning. Good application of knowledge and understanding
                                       of concepts/issues mostly relevant to the context.

                      1        1–2     Analysis of discrete points of the economic evidence with no chain of
                                       reasoning attempted. Basic application of knowledge and
                                       understanding of concepts/issues with limited relevance to the context.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Indicative content:
                    • Airlines: fuel is a large proportion of airline costs; lower oil prices reduce production
                      costs
                    • airlines may pass cost savings on as lower ticket prices → rise in demand for air
                      travel
                    • airlines may increase profit margins instead of cutting prices
                    • Road haulage: diesel is a major variable cost; lower prices reduce costs per mile
                    • hauliers may cut freight rates → cheaper delivery costs for retailers → possible
                      lower shop prices
                    • Wider economy: lower transport costs may reduce inflation; may reduce incentives
                      to switch to electric vehicles
                    • use of data: oil fell from $120 to $75, a fall of about 37.5% · a very significant cost
                      reduction
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     5
                                                    MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  16               State two features of a market operating under monopolistic competition.                    2

                   AO1 = 2

                   1 mark for each feature (max 2).

                   Possible answers:
                    • a large number of relatively small firms
                    • product differentiation (slightly different products, e.g. branding)
                    • low barriers to entry and exit
                    • some degree of price-setting power for each firm (not a pure price taker)
                    • non-price competition (advertising, branding)
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  17               Explain one possible reason why professional footballers in the Premier                     2
                   League tend to earn much higher salaries than nurses in the NHS.

                   AO1 = 2

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      2         2      Possible reason explained.

                      1         1      Possible reason identified.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • supply of elite football talent is extremely limited (rare natural ability)
                    • demand for top football talent from clubs is very high (global TV revenue)
                    • clubs compete internationally for the best players → bidding up wages
                    • nurses are in plentiful supply (more trained each year) → downward pressure on
                      pay
                    • NHS is a monopsony employer, limiting the ability of nurses to bargain for higher
                      pay
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     6
 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  18               Explain one possible reason why the demand for petrol in the short run is                   2
                   likely to be price inelastic.

                   AO1 = 2

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      2         2      Possible reason explained.

                      1         1      Possible reason identified.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • petrol is a necessity for drivers commuting to work / doing the school run
                    • few close substitutes in the short run · drivers cannot switch cars overnight
                    • addictive / habitual nature of car use
                    • public transport alternatives may not be practical for many drivers in the short run
                    • a clear definition of 'price inelastic' without context = 1 mark
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     7
                                                    MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  19      1        Using Table 3, calculate the weekly productivity of the workforce of Priya's                2
                   Pottery. Give your answer to the nearest whole number.

                   AO2 = 2
                    • 260 ÷ 8 = 32.5 (1) vases per worker per week
                    • To nearest whole number = 33 (1) vases per worker per week

                   2 marks for correct answer to nearest whole number with no working shown.

                   1 mark for correct answer not rounded to the nearest whole number.


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  19      2        Explain one benefit of increased productivity to a business such as Priya's                 2
                   Pottery.

                   AO1 = 2

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      2         2      Possible benefit explained.

                      1         1      Possible benefit identified.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • lower average cost per vase → higher profit margin
                    • higher output without hiring more workers → can meet rising demand
                    • greater competitiveness against rival potteries → more orders
                    • increased profit can be reinvested (new kiln, training)
                    • ability to lower prices and still make a profit → larger market share
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     8
 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                Total
                                                                                                   marks

  20               Draw and label demand and supply curves on the graph from the table.               3
                   Clearly identify the equilibrium price and quantity.

                   AO2 = 3
                    • 1 mark for correct drawing and labelling of the demand curve.
                    • 1 mark for correct drawing and labelling of the supply curve.
                    • 1 mark for correctly identifying the equilibrium price £30 and equilibrium quantity
                      18 units.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                            9
                                                    MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  21               Using Figure 2 and Table 4, assess whether the benefits of growth for a                     9
                   business such as Mia's Mechanics exceed the costs.

                   AO2 = 3, AO3 = 6

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      3        7–9     A fully justified conclusion with sustained evaluation, coherent and
                                       relevant, with judgements supported by evidence. A full analysis of the
                                       economic evidence, a well-developed and logical chain of reasoning.
                                       Comprehensive application of knowledge and understanding of
                                       pertinent concepts/issues relevant to the context; specialist language
                                       used throughout.

                      2        4–6     A partially justified conclusion with an evaluation, mostly coherent and
                                       relevant; judgements may not be fully supported by evidence. An
                                       incomplete analysis of the economic evidence; starts to develop a
                                       chain of reasoning. Good application of knowledge and understanding
                                       of concepts/issues mostly relevant to the context; specialist language
                                       used mostly appropriately.

                      1        1–3     A basic conclusion or assertion may be present based on evaluative
                                       judgements supported by little or no evidence. Analysis of discrete
                                       points of the economic evidence with no chain of reasoning attempted.
                                       Basic application of knowledge and understanding of concepts/issues
                                       with limited relevance to the context; may use non-specialist language.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Indicative content:
                    • Benefits (economies of scale): reduced long-run average cost per car serviced
                    • purchasing economies · bulk discounts from parts suppliers (mentioned in Figure
                      2)
                    • technical economies · specialist diagnostic equipment only affordable at larger
                      scale
                    • managerial and financial economies as the business grows
                    • Data: AC at 200 = £150; AC at 400 = £130; AC at 600 = £110 per car → clear fall
                      (economies of scale)
                    • Costs (diseconomies of scale): communication problems and slow decision-
                      making across three sites (Figure 2)
                    • coordination costs rise as the business grows beyond a certain size
                    • possible fall in motivation / consistency of service quality
                    • Data: AC at 800 = £130 per car → rise from £110 (diseconomies setting in between
                      600 and 800)
                    • Evaluation: benefits exceed costs up to about 600 cars per month; beyond that,
                      growth harms the business; optimum scale seems to be around 600
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     10
                                                    MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  22               Define the term 'market failure'.                                                           2

                   AO1 = 2

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      2         2      Complete definition of market failure.

                      1         1      Partial definition of market failure.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • a situation where the free market fails to allocate resources efficiently, leading to
                      loss of welfare (2 marks)
                    • when the market, on its own, produces an outcome that is not in society's best
                      interest · for example, too much of a harmful good or too little of a beneficial one (2
                      marks)
                    • when markets do not work well (1 mark)
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  23               Calculate the percentage change in the UK electric vehicle share of new car                 3
                   sales between 2018 and 2022. Give your answer to the nearest whole %.

                   AO2 = 3
                    • 2018 ≈ 1.6%; 2022 ≈ 16.6%
                    • (16.6 – 1.6) ÷ 1.6 × 100 = 937.5% ≈ 938%

                   Accept any answer between 900% and 975%, using values from acceptable range.

                   1 mark for correct method but wrong answer.

                   1 mark for correctly identifying values from the graph for both years within acceptable
                   ranges.

                   2 marks if not rounded to nearest whole %.

                   3 marks for a correct answer with no working shown.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     11
                                                    MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  24               Analyse the possible positive externalities that may result from an increase in             6
                   the number of electric vehicles on UK roads.

                   AO2 = 6

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      3        5–6     A full analysis of the economic evidence, a well-developed and logical
                                       chain of reasoning.

                      2        3–4     An incomplete analysis of the economic evidence, starts to develop a
                                       chain of reasoning. Good application of knowledge and understanding
                                       of concepts/issues mostly relevant to the context.

                      1        1–2     Analysis of discrete points of the economic evidence with no chain of
                                       reasoning attempted. Basic application of knowledge and
                                       understanding of concepts/issues with limited relevance to the context.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • reduced local air pollution · better health outcomes, reduced NHS costs
                    • lower carbon emissions · slows climate change, benefits future generations
                    • quieter roads · reduced noise pollution in urban areas
                    • reduced dependence on imported oil · greater energy security
                    • growth in UK battery manufacturing and associated high-skilled jobs
                    • development of a charging-point network benefits all EV drivers, not just the buyer
                    • demonstration effect · more EVs on the road encourages others to buy
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     12
                                                    MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  25               Explain two possible disadvantages of the UK remaining heavily reliant on                   6
                   petrol and diesel vehicles.

                   AO2 = 2, AO3 = 4

                   Apply the following mark scheme for each disadvantage.

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      3         3      A full and precise explanation applying economic concepts and issues.

                      2         2      A partial explanation applying economic concepts and issues but
                                       lacking detail.

                      1         1      Fragmented points made.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • high levels of urban air pollution (NO₂, particulates) → negative externalities on
                      public health and the NHS
                    • continued high carbon emissions → increased long-run costs of climate change
                    • heavy reliance on imported oil → vulnerability to global oil price shocks and
                      geopolitical risk
                    • UK misses out on jobs and growth in the fast-expanding global EV/battery supply
                      chain
                    • failure to meet 2035 targets → potential reputational damage and future regulatory
                      costs
                    • persistent negative externalities justify government intervention (taxation)
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     13
                   MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                  14
 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                        Total
                                                                                                           marks

  26               Do you think the UK government should subsidise the purchase of new                       15
                   electric vehicles in the UK? Use Items A and B and your own economic
                   knowledge to justify your view.

                   AO2 = 5, AO3 = 10

                    Level   Marks     Description

                      5     13–15     A justified conclusion with sustained evaluation, coherent and relevant,
                                      with judgements fully supported by evidence. Analysis of a relevant
                                      selection of the economic evidence, with a well-developed logical
                                      chain of reasoning. Comprehensive application of knowledge and
                                      understanding of pertinent concepts/issues relevant to the context;
                                      effective use of specialist language maintained throughout.

                      4     10–12     A partially justified conclusion with an evaluation, mostly coherent and
                                      relevant; judgements may not be fully supported by evidence. Analysis
                                      of a relevant selection of the economic evidence; a logical chain of
                                      reasoning established but not fully developed. Strong application of
                                      knowledge and understanding of a broad range of concepts/issues
                                      relevant to the context; mostly effective use of specialist language
                                      throughout.

                      3      7–9      A partially justified conclusion with an evaluation, but not integrated in
                                      a coherent way; judgements are not fully supported by evidence.
                                      Analysis of a relevant selection of the economic evidence and starts to
                                      develop a chain of reasoning. Good application of knowledge and
                                      understanding of a broad range of concepts/issues relevant to the
                                      context; consistent use of specialist language but not always effective.

                      2      4–6      A basic conclusion is present based on simple evaluative judgements
                                      supported by little or no evidence. Analysis of a relevant selection of
                                      the economic evidence with no chain of reasoning attempted. Simple
                                      application of knowledge and understanding of a narrow range of
                                      concepts/issues relevant to the context; sporadic use of specialist
                                      language.

                      1      1–3      Evaluative judgements are simple with no conclusion present. Analysis
                                      of a limited selection of the economic evidence with no chain of
                                      reasoning attempted; selection of evidence may lack relevance. Basic
                                      application of knowledge and understanding of concepts/issues with
                                      limited relevance to the context; uses non-specialist language.

                              0       Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Indicative content:

                   Yes (in favour of subsidy):

                    • EVs generate positive externalities (cleaner air, lower emissions) that the private
                      market will under-produce without intervention
                    • use of data: PED = –1.8 → highly price-sensitive, so subsidies should cause a large
                      rise in demand
                    • supports the 2035 petrol/diesel ban target (Item A)


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                   15
                    • improves access for lower-income households who are currently priced out (Item B)
                    • stimulates UK battery manufacturing and charging-point investment

                   No (against subsidy):

                    • opportunity cost · money could be spent on the NHS, schools, or public transport
                      (Item B)
                    • subsidies mostly benefit wealthier households who buy new cars (Item B)
                    • environmental problems with battery raw materials (lithium, cobalt) in producing
                      countries
                    • subsidies may not work unless charging infrastructure is also expanded (Item A: 53
                      000 vs. 300 000 needed)
                    • could encourage over-reliance on imports from Chinese EV manufacturers

                   Overall judgement:

                    • depends on the size and targeting of the subsidy (e.g. means-tested, or for used
                      EVs only)
                    • subsidy combined with charging-point investment is likely to be more effective than
                      either alone
                    • trade-off between short-run cost to taxpayer and long-run environmental / economic
                      gains
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.
